exemplar.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Maintains a clear focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay.
Effectively links both History and Human Sciences to the central question.
Uses a range of concrete, apt examples (e.g., Pompeii DNA findings, Yemen literacy statistics, Mercator projection).
Demonstrates some awareness of differing perspectives and acknowledges counter-arguments.
weaknesses: Analysis often remains descriptive and assertive, with key claims insufficiently justified or oversimplified.
Limited evaluation of counter-arguments and insufficient probing of the implications of main arguments for knowledge production.
Conceptual distinctions (truth vs. validity, model vs. reality, bias vs. contradiction) are blurred, reducing analytical rigor.
Transitions between sections (especially moving from History to Human Sciences) are abrupt, affecting overall cohesion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs mark of 4 out of 10 must be reflected in the criterion âUnderstanding knowledge questions and focus on the titleâ. Although the essay shows clear focus, relevant AOK links and solid examples, the analysis is predominantly descriptive, with oversimplified claims and minimal evaluation of counter-arguments. These limitations align the work with band 3â4 (âConnected to the title with superficial AOK links, largely descriptive, limited argument supportâ). A score of 4 is therefore appropriate to reflect its solid but underdeveloped reasoning and the lack of sustained critical engagement.

annotations.comment: The Yemen literacy example is contextually rich and shows awareness of how indicators can misrepresent educational quality, strengthening the humanâscience discussion.
While the Homo Economicus discussion is insightful, labeling the model as âcompletely falseâ is too absolute; consider more balanced language to account for its heuristic value.
The blanket assertion that âcontradictory evidence does not exist in Historyâ oversimplifies debates in historiography and should be qualified or countered with opposing scholarly views.
Incorporating the studentâs own History IA on Hiroshima/Nagasaki demonstrates engagement with extended research and provides real evidence of diverse historical interpretations.
The claim that a perfect predictive model would remove free will is intriguing but needs theoretical support and clearer linkage to ethical frameworks.
Noting that model accuracy must be critically assessed before ethical application is a strong metacognitive move and demonstrates reflective thinking.

The student presents a clear, concise thesis by explicitly stating the central argument that historians and human scientists do not have an ethical obligation to follow the directive âdo not ignore contradictory evidence.â

The definition of âethical obligationâ is too general; the student should specify what constitutes an ethical duty in knowledge production and link it directly to historical and humanâscience contexts.
Strong conceptual point that historical knowledge is malleable and evolves with new evidence, framing the ethical discussion around interpretation rather than outright invalidation.
The discussion of source limitations in history is descriptive but fails to connect these constraints to any ethical considerations, weakening the link between context and obligation.
Good recognition that historical knowledge is contextâdependent and remains âtrueâ within the constraints of its original sources, demonstrating nuanced understanding of knowledge frameworks.
The Pompeii DNA case is an apt, concrete example that illustrates how new evidence refines rather than negates earlier interpretations, enriching the historical analysis.
The conclusion reiterates ideas but stops short of a decisive evaluation of whether ethical obligations should be followed, leaving the essayâs judgment ambiguous.
Reiteration of the thesis midâessay is redundant and disrupts narrative flow; consider moving this material to the introduction or refining its placement.
The contrast between objective truth and inherited bias requires clearer definitions; delineating âbiasâ versus âcontradictionâ would sharpen the argument.
Acknowledging both overt and subtle forms of historian bias (nationalist vs. linguistic) adds valuable nuance to the discussion of ethical obligations.
The Mercator map example effectively ties ethical obligation to the choice of models, showing how ignoring contradictions can perpetuate harmful worldviews.
The shift to the human sciences is abrupt; a clear transitional sentence outlining the change in AOK would improve reader guidance.
The reference to Ghanaâs debt crisis appears truncated and underdeveloped; expanding this example or correcting the text would clarify its relevance.
The discussion of Homo Economicus demonstrates strong understanding of simplified modelling in the human sciences and its implications for ethical choice.
Conceptual distinctions between truth, validity, modelâreality relations and bias versus contradiction are blurred; clearer definitions would enhance analytical rigor.
The historiographical debate on whether contradictory evidence ever truly negates past knowledge is complex; invoking additional scholars would avoid oversimplification.
Counterarguments (e.g., traditionalist vs revisionist) are noted but not fully evaluated; the student should probe their implications and relative merits more deeply.
Relies heavily on a single Foucault citation; integrating further theoretical perspectives on power and knowledge would strengthen the ethical analysis.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
